subject: Mathematics
q:
Let p,q,r be three logical statements. Consider the compound statements
S1:~p∨q∨~p∨r and S2:p→q∨r
Then, which of the following is NOT true?
question:
Let p,q,r be three logical statements. Consider the compound statements
S1:~p∨q∨~p∨r and S2:p→q∨r
Then, which of the following is NOT true?
solution:
S1:~p∨q∨~p∨r

≡~p∨q∨r

S2:p→q∨r≡~p∨q∨r

So S1 is equivalent to S2

subject: Mathematics
q: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to :
question: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to :
solution: Since truth value of $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ and $p \rightarrow(p \vee q)$ are same, hence $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to $p \rightarrow(p \vee q)$.

solution:

Negation of $p \rightarrow q$ is $\sim(p \rightarrow q)=p \wedge \sim q$

q: The contrapositive of the statement "If I reach the station in time, then I will catch the train" is
question: The contrapositive of the statement "If I reach the station in time, then I will catch the train" is
options: If I do not reach the station in time, then I will catch the train.
If do not reach the station in time, then I will not catch the train.
If I will catch the train, then I reach the station in time.
If I will not catch the train, then I do not reach the station in time.
solution:
Contrapositive of p→q is ~q→~p.

⇒The statement will be "If I will not catch the train, then I do not reach the station in time".

subject: Mathematics
q: The contrapositive of the statement “If you are born in India, then you are a citizen of India”, is
question: The contrapositive of the statement “If you are born in India, then you are a citizen of India”, is
options: If you are not born in India, then you are not a citizen of India.
If you are a citizen of India, then you are born in India.
If you are born in India, then you are not a citizen of India.
If you are not a citizen of India, then you are not born in India.
solution:
As we know, contrapositive of p→q is ∼q→∼p
Hence, required contrapositive statement is “If you are not a citizen of India, then you are not born in India”.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>r</mi></math> be three statements such that the truth value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> . Then the truth values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>r</mi></math> be three statements such that the truth value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> . Then the truth values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively :
solution:
Given that the truth value of p∧q→~q∨r is F .

For the above truth value to be possible, p∧q should be TRUE and ~q∨r should be FALSE.

p∧q is TRUE when p & q both are TRUE.

~q∨r is FALSE when q is TRUE and r is FALSE.

So, the truth values of p, q, r are T, T, F respectively.

q: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">~</mo><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo mathvariant="italic">∨</mo><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is not equivalent to :

solution:

A B ~A ~A∨B B⇒~A∨B
T T

F

F T T T T
F F T T T

It is a tautology.

Truth table of given options:

A⇒B ~A⇒B B⇒A⇒B A⇒~A⇒B B⇒~A⇒B
T T T T T
F T T T T
T T T T T
T F T T T

Option A: B⇒A⇒B is a tautology.

Option C: A⇒~A⇒B is a tautology.

Option D: B⇒~A⇒B is a tautology.

Hence, it is very clear that given statement is not equivalent to option B.

P : If 7 is an odd number, then 7 is divisible by 2.

Q : If 7 is a prime number, then 7 is an odd number.

If V1 is the truth value of the contrapositive of P and V2 is the truth value of contrapositive of Q, then the ordered pair V1,V2 equals
question:
Consider the following two statements:

P : If 7 is an odd number, then 7 is divisible by 2.

Q : If 7 is a prime number, then 7 is an odd number.

If V1 is the truth value of the contrapositive of P and V2 is the truth value of contrapositive of Q, then the ordered pair V1,V2 equals
solution:
Contrapositive of P:

7 is not divisible by 2⇒7 is not odd number

T ⇒F:FV1

Contrapositive of Q:

7 is not odd number ⇒7 is not a prime number  

F⇒ F:T V2

Thus, V1,V2≡F,T

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>e</mi></math>, then the truth values of <math><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively, (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>e</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>e</mi></math>)
question: If <math><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>e</mi></math>, then the truth values of <math><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively, (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>e</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>e</mi></math>)
solution:
We know that the conditional statement a⇒b is False only when a is False and b is True.

Hence, p⇒(q∨r): False when p:True;  q∨r : False

Again, we know that a∨b is False when both a & b are false.

Hence, p:True; q:False; r: False.

subject: Mathematics
q: The negation of the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is logically equivalent to
question: The negation of the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is logically equivalent to
solution:
To find negation of (~q∧p→~p∨q

Let ~q∧p=A & ~p∨q=B

~A→B   =~~A∨B =A∧~B

Using above formula be get

~q∧p∧~~p∨q  =~q∧p∧p∧~q

=A∧A  =A=~q∧p =p∧~q or ~p→q

subject: Mathematics
q: If $(\mathrm{p} \wedge \sim \mathrm{q}) \wedge(\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{r}) \rightarrow \sim p \vee q$ is false, then the truth values of $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}$ and $\mathrm{r}$ are respectively
question: If $(\mathrm{p} \wedge \sim \mathrm{q}) \wedge(\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{r}) \rightarrow \sim p \vee q$ is false, then the truth values of $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}$ and $\mathrm{r}$ are respectively
solution: As the truth table for the $(p \wedge \sim q) \wedge(p \wedge r)$ $\rightarrow \sim p \vee q$ is false, then only possible values of $(p, q, r)$ is $(T, F, T)$ $\begin{array}{llllll}p & q & r & \sim q & p \wedge \sim q & p \wedge r \\ \sim p & \sim p \vee q & (p \wedge \sim q) \wedge(p \wedge r)(p \wedge \sim q) \wedge(p \wedge r) \\ \rightarrow \sim p \vee q\end{array}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Statement-1: The statement $A \rightarrow(B \rightarrow A)$ is equivalent to $A \rightarrow(A \vee B)$.

Statement-2: The statement $\sim[(\mathrm{A} \wedge \mathrm{B}) \rightarrow$ $(\sim \mathrm{A} \vee \mathrm{B})]$ is a Tautology.
question:
Statement-1: The statement $A \rightarrow(B \rightarrow A)$ is equivalent to $A \rightarrow(A \vee B)$.

Statement-2: The statement $\sim[(\mathrm{A} \wedge \mathrm{B}) \rightarrow$ $(\sim \mathrm{A} \vee \mathrm{B})]$ is a Tautology.
options: Statement- 1 is false; Statement- 2 is true.
Statement-1 is true; Statement-2 is true; Statement- 2 is not correct explanation for Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true; Statement- 2 is false.
Statement-1 is true; Statement-2 is true; Statement- 2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following statements is a tautology?
question: Which of the following statements is a tautology?
solution:
Plotting the truth table of the following option we have,

p q ~p ~q ~p∨q
T T F F T
T F F T F
F T T F T
F F T T T

We know that, A⇒B is only false when A is true and B is false, now using this rule and above table we will make truth table of options,

~p∨q⇒p p⇒~p∨q ~p∨q⇒q q⇒~p∨q
T T T T
T F T T
F T T T
F T F T

 

Hence q⇒~p∨q is tautology.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Among the statements

S1: p⇒q∨~p∧q is a tautology

S2: q⇒p⇒~p∧q is a contradiction
question:
Among the statements

S1: p⇒q∨~p∧q is a tautology

S2: q⇒p⇒~p∧q is a contradiction
solution:
Plotting the Truth table of the given expression we get,

p q ~p ~p∧q p⇒q q⇒p p⇒q∨~p∧q q⇒p∨~p∧q
T T F F T T T T
T F F F F T F T
F T T T T F T T
F F T F T T T T

Hence, q⇒p∨~p∧q is a tautology, and p⇒q∨~p∧q is neither tautology and contradiction,

So both statements are false.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Among the two statements

S1:p⇒q∧p∧~q is a contradiction and S2:p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q is a tautology
question:
Among the two statements

S1:p⇒q∧p∧~q is a contradiction and S2:p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q is a tautology
solution:
Given,

S1:p⇒q∧p∧~q

≡~p∨q∧p∧~q as A⇒B≡~A∨B

≡~p∧p∧~q∨q∧p∧~q

≡f∧~q∨f∧p

≡f∨f≡f

 

S2:p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q

≡p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q

≡p∨~p∧q∨p∨~p∧~q

≡t∧q∨t∧~q≡q∨~q≡t

Hence, both statement are true.

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following is equivalent to the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math>
question: Which of the following is equivalent to the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
Which of the following is equivalent to the Boolean expression p∧~q ?

For option 1

~p→~q≡p∨~q

≢p∧~q 

For Option 2

~q→p≡~~q∨p≡q∧~p

≢p∧~q

For option 3

~(p→q)

≡~(~p∨q)

≡(p∧~q)

For option 4

~p→~q≡~~p∨~q≡p∧q

≢p∧~q

subject: Mathematics
q: Negation of the Boolean statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to:
question: Negation of the Boolean statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to:
solution:
Given,

p∨q⇒~r∨p

≡~p∨q∨~r∨p

≡~p∧~q∨p∨~r

≡~p∨p∧~q∨p∨~r

≡~q∨p∨~r

So negation of~q∨p∨~r  is ~p∧q∧r.

q: The Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to:

p q ~p ~q p∧~q q∨~p (p∧~q)
⇒(q∨~p)		 p⇒q
T F F T T F F F
F T T F F T T T
T T F F F T T T
F F T T F T T T

∴ p∧~q⇒q∨~p

≡p⇒q

q: The proposition <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mo>~</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to :

solution:
~p∧~q

=~p∨~~q By De Morgan's Laws.

=~p∨q

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the statement "The match will be played only if the weather is good and ground is not wet". Select the correct negation from the following:
question: Consider the statement "The match will be played only if the weather is good and ground is not wet". Select the correct negation from the following:
options: The match will not be played and weather is not good and ground is wet.
If the match will not be played, then either weather is not good or ground is wet.
The match will be played and weather is not good or ground is wet.
The match will not be played or weather is good and ground is not wet.
solution:
Let p: weather is good
and q: ground is not wet

Given statement "The match will be played only if the weather is good and ground is not wet" can be written as p∧q

Now, negation of  p∧q is ~p∧q≡~p∨~q

which is equivalent to "The match will be played and weather is not good or ground is wet".

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $p$ and $q$ be any two logical statements and $\mathrm{r}: \mathrm{p} \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$. If $r$ has a truth value $F$, then the truth values of $p$ and $q$ are respectively:
question: Let $p$ and $q$ be any two logical statements and $\mathrm{r}: \mathrm{p} \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$. If $r$ has a truth value $F$, then the truth values of $p$ and $q$ are respectively:
solution:
$p \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$ has truth value $\mathrm{F}$.
It means $p \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$ is false.
It means $p$ is true and $\sim p \vee q$ is false.
$\Rightarrow p$ is true and both $\sim p$ and $q$ are false.
$\Rightarrow p$ is true and $q$ is false.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the following statements:

P: Ramu is intelligent.

Q: Ramu is rich.

R: Ramu is not honest.

The negation of the statement "Ramu is intelligent and honest if and only if Ramu is not rich" can be expressed as:
question:
Consider the following statements:

P: Ramu is intelligent.

Q: Ramu is rich.

R: Ramu is not honest.

The negation of the statement "Ramu is intelligent and honest if and only if Ramu is not rich" can be expressed as:
solution:
Given,

P: Ramu is intelligent

Q: Ramu is rich

R: Ramu is not honest

Given statement, "Ramu is intelligent and honest if and only if Ramu is not rich"

=P∧~R⇔~Q

So, negation of the statement is ~P∧~R⇔~Q

=~~P∧~R∨~Q∧Q∨P∧~R

[using A⇔B=~A∨B∧~B∨A]

=P∧~R∧Q∨~Q∧~P∨R

[by using~A∨B=~A∧~B and ~A∧B=~A∨~B]

solution:

~p∨~p∧q

Now we know that,

By demorgan law, ~A∨B≡~A∧~B

Now using above formula we get,
